items.foco: Pediculosis capitis due to Pediculus humanus capitis
items.title: Pediculus humanus capitis
items.contentHtml:
Pediculosis capitis

  • Pediculus humanus capitis is the head louse, obligate hematophagous, which deposits nits (eggs) 1-2 mm from the base of the hair shaft.
  • Infestation is more common in children aged 3-11 years; the itching is due to a hypersensitivity reaction to the parasite's saliva.
  • The diagnosis is confirmed by visualizing adult lice or viable nits (opaque, firmly attached) with a magnifying glass or Wood's light.
  • Key fact: empty nits are translucent and located >1 cm from the scalp, indicating an old or treated infestation.

items.foco: Pediculosis corporis due to Pediculus humanus corporis
items.title: Pediculus humanus corporis
items.contentHtml:
Pediculosis corporis

  • Pediculus humanus corporis lives on clothing and only passes to the skin to feed; It is a vector of exanthematous typhus (Rickettsia prowazekii), relapsing fever (Borrelia recurrentis) and trench fever (Bartonella quintana).
  • Intense itching leads to linear excoriations on the trunk and shoulders; Lice and nits are found in the seams of clothing, not on the body.
  • The main treatment is personal hygiene and washing clothes at >60°C; Routine topical skin treatment is not required.

items.foco: Pediculosis pubis due to Pthirus pubis
items.title: Pthirus pubis
items.contentHtml:
Pediculosis pubis

  • Pthirus pubis (crab) has a wider and shorter body, with legs adapted to thick hairs; It affects the pubes, armpits, eyebrows and eyelashes.
  • Predominantly sexual transmission; pruritus is the guiding symptom, with cerulean macules (maculae caeruleae) due to punctate hemorrhage at the bite site.
  • First-line treatment: permethrin 1% in lotion or cream, applied to affected areas and left to act for 10 minutes; repeat after 7 days.
  • Sexual contacts should be treated and other STIs ruled out.

items.foco: Intense itching as the main symptom
items.title: intense itching
items.contentHtml:
Intense itching

  • Itching in pediculosis is caused by type intravenous hypersensitivity to louse saliva; It may take 2-6 weeks to appear in primary infestation.
  • In recurrent infestations, itching appears in 24-48 hours due to immunological memory.
  • Nocturnal itching is characteristic of greater activity of the parasite with body heat.
  • Do not confuse with psychogenic itching or atopic dermatitis; The presence of nits attached confirms the diagnosis.

items.foco: Excoriations and scabs on the scalp
items.title: Excoriations and scabs
items.contentHtml:
Excoriations and scabs

  • Linear excoriations on the scalp, neck and retroauricular region are secondary to scratching and can be superinfected with Staphylococcus aureus or Streptococcus pyogenes.
  • The presence of meliceric scabs suggests secondary impetigo; it should be treated with topical antibiotic (mupirocin) or systemic if there is lymphadenopathy.
  • Differential diagnosis: seborrheic dermatitis (greasy scales, diffuse erythema, without nits) and scalp psoriasis (well-demarcated erythematosquamous plaques).

items.foco: Nits attached to the hair shaft
items.title: Nits attached
items.contentHtml:
Attached nits

  • Nits are 0.8 mm, oval, grayish-white louse eggs, attached to the hair shaft with chitinous cement that is resistant to washing.
  • They are located <1 cm from the base of the scalp in active infestation; at greater distances they indicate past or treated infestation.
  • Key difference with dandruff: nits do not come off with brushing; The dandruff comes off easily and is not attached.
  • Wood's light fluoresces viable nits in a yellowish-white color.

items.title: direct transmission
items.contentHtml:
Transmission by direct contact

  • The transmission of Pediculus humanus capitis is by direct head-to-head contact; The louse does not fly or jump, it moves by walking.
  • Infection by fomites (combs, hats, pillows) is rare because the louse dehydrates and dies within 24-48 hours outside the host.
  • Pets are not a reservoir; Human lice are species specific.
  • Infestation is not associated with lack of hygiene; It occurs at all socioeconomic levels.

items.title: Permethrin 1%
items.contentHtml:
Topical treatment with 1% permethrin

  • 1% permethrin is the first-line treatment for pediculosis capitis and pubis; It is a pyrethroid that alters the sodium conductance in the parasite's membrane channels.
  • Apply to dry scalp, leave for 10 minutes and rinse; repeat after 7-10 days to eliminate lice hatched from viable nits.
  • Contraindicated in allergies to chrysanthemums; safety in pregnancy category B.
  • Alternatives: topical ivermectin 0.5% (lotion, 10 minutes) or malathion 0.5% (lotion, 8-12 hours, toxic if ingested).

items.foco: Resistance to permethrin using oral ivermectin
items.title: Permethrin resistance
items.contentHtml:
Permethrin resistance

  • Resistance to 1% permethrin is suspected if there is no response after two complete cycles; mediated by mutations in the kdr gene (knockdown resistance).
  • In case of resistance, the alternative is oral ivermectin at a dose of 200-400 mcg/kg, repeat after 7-14 days.
  • Oral ivermectin is contraindicated in children <15 kg and in pregnancy (category C).
  • Another option: 1% lindane (lotion, 4 minutes) but with risk of neurotoxicity, reserved for failure of other therapies.

items.title: Eyelid pediculosis
items.contentHtml:
Eyelash pediculosis

  • Eyelash pediculosis is caused by Pthirus pubis in adults or Pediculus humanus capitis in children.
  • The treatment of choice is vaseline (occlusion) applied to eyelashes twice a day for 7-10 days; suffocates lice and nits.
  • Do not use topical permethrin or ivermectin near the eyes due to the risk of corneal irritation.
  • Nits should be manually removed with tweezers; In extensive cases, oral ivermectin (200 mcg/kg) is a safe alternative.

items.foco: Does not require school exclusion if adequate treatment
items.title: School exclusion
items.contentHtml:
Does not require school exclusion

  • According to the American Academy of Pediatrics, a child with pediculosis capitis should not be excluded from school if he or she has received appropriate treatment (1% permethrin or equivalent).
  • No-nit policies have been discouraged because they cause unnecessary absenteeism and do not reduce transmission.
  • Return is permitted. immediately after the first treatment; no medical certificate is required.
  • Reinfestation is common through untreated contacts; Parents should be educated and close contacts reviewed.

items.title: Differential diagnosis
items.contentHtml:
Differential diagnosis with seborrheic dermatitis

  • Seborrheic dermatitis: greasy, yellowish scales, diffuse erythema on the scalp, eyebrows, nasolabial folds; there are no nits attached.
  • In pediculosis, the nits are firmly attached to the hair shaft; in seborrheic dermatitis the scaling comes off easily.
  • Other differential diagnoses: psoriasis (well-demarcated erythematosquamous plaques, Auspitz sign), tinea capitis (desquamative alopecia, green fluorescence with Wood's light, positive culture for dermatophytes).
  • The diagnostic key is the visualization of the louse or nit with magnifying glass.
